title: Gini Impurity: Another Measure of Pureness
concept: Gini impurity is a metric that measures how often a randomly chosen element from a group would be incorrectly labeled if it were randomly labeled according to the distribution of labels in the group.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does an entropy score of 0 mean?

options.text: The group is completely pure, containing only one category of data.
The group is highly messy and split 50/50.
The model has failed to train.

exercises.explain: Correct! Entropy of 0 represents absolute purity.

cards.title: Picking candy in the dark
cards.body: Imagine a bowl with 90 red candies and 10 blue candies. You pull one out — it's red. You put it back, shake the bowl, and pull another. Because the bowl is mostly red, you'll probably pull red again. But what if the bowl was 50/50? The chance of guessing wrong increases. Gini impurity measures this probability of making a mistake.

cards.title: Gini Impurity: Purity by probability
cards.body: Gini impurity is a score from 0 to 0.5. Like entropy, a Gini impurity of **0** means the group is completely pure. A Gini impurity of **0.5** means the group is a perfect 50/50 mix (maximum impurity).

cards.title: Labeling by guess
cards.body: If you are handed a bag of socks where 90% are white and 10% are black, and you are forced to label them by guessing, you'll guess 'white' most of the time and be correct 90% of the time. The Gini impurity measures the chance of your random label mismatching the actual sock.

cards.title: Calculating Gini
cards.body: For a pure group (100% spam), Gini is **0.0**. For a 90/10 split, Gini is **0.18** (low impurity). For a 50/50 split, Gini peaks at **0.5** (maximum impurity). Decision trees use Gini or Entropy as their criteria to evaluate splits.

cards.title: Gini and Entropy are mostly identical in practice
cards.body: You might wonder if you must always use one over the other. In practice, Gini and Entropy lead to almost identical trees. Gini impurity is slightly faster to calculate because it doesn't require computing logarithmic functions, which is why it is often the default choice in tree libraries.

cards.title: Gini Impurity Scale
cards.body: Purity levels scored under the Gini metric:

left.text:
All items are the same category.
Zero chance of incorrect random labeling.
Perfect purity.

right.text:
Items split 50/50 between classes.
Maximum chance of incorrect labeling.
Peak impurity.

exercises.prompt: What is the maximum possible value for Gini impurity (representing a perfect 50/50 mix)?
exercises.explain: Correct! Gini impurity ranges from 0 (pure) to 0.5 (perfect 50/50 mix).

exercises.prompt: True or false: A Gini impurity of 0.0 means the group has maximum disorder.
exercises.explain: False! A Gini impurity of 0.0 means the group is completely pure, containing only one category.

exercises.sentence: Gini impurity is often preferred over entropy because it is slightly ___ to calculate.
exercises.explain: Gini impurity doesn't require complex logarithmic math, making it computationally faster to run.

exercises.prompt: Which of these splits has a Gini impurity of exactly 0.0?

options.text: A bowl containing 20 green apples and 0 yellow bananas
A bowl containing 10 green apples and 10 yellow bananas
A bowl containing 15 green apples and 5 yellow bananas

exercises.explain: Correct! The bowl is 100% pure apples, so its Gini impurity is exactly 0.0.

exercises.prompt: A decision tree evaluates two potential questions to split a group of 100 items. Question A results in two groups with a Gini impurity of 0.1. Question B results in two groups with a Gini impurity of 0.4. Which question should the tree choose, and why?

options.text: Question A, because it creates groups with lower Gini impurity (higher purity).
Question B, because a higher Gini score represents a better, more disordered mix.
Either question, since Gini scores don't affect tree splits.
Question B, because 0.4 is closer to 0.5.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The goal of a decision tree is to split data into the purest possible groups. Since Question A results in a lower Gini impurity (0.1 vs. 0.4), it is the superior choice. Next, let's learn how we measure this drop in impurity!
